content:
LTO Rust modules with C helpers

I finally got some time to investigate about possible ways that we can LTO helpers together with Rust kernel modules at a crate level.

Here are 3 possibilities:

  1. Have the LLVM IR of the helpers somehow available to rustc and then it gets used in compilation step.
  2. For each crate, invoke rustc, and then link it with helpers, and then use the object file for the final linking.
  3. Use linker plugin LTO and do a global LTO.

We previously explored option 3 only. @Andreas Hindborg reported that kernel doesn't boot on real machine with this, and @Alice Ryhl reported global LTO is not preferred because it's slow and offers little gain.

I looked into option 1 today and I don't think there're any hacks that we can use to ask Rust to embed arbitrary LLVM IR/BC for compilation. It seems that if rustc is doing the linker, we can trick it into using non-Rust bitcode by make the file name end with .rcgu.o, but since we use --emit, rustc wouldn't run the linker stage at all (plus, this is a horrible hack).

So option 2 looks like the only remaining solution. However since we need link helper multiple times, it will violate one-definition rule (ODR) and cause linker error on duplicate symbol. Using weak linkage avoids the ODR issue, but it also inhibits the inlining (because the symbol can be overriden)

There is one way to workaround the issue, which is to use linkonce_odr. This is entirely LLVM internal and is not exposed to C code, but we can use some hacks:

  1. For helpers, define them using extern inline. This allows an inline hint to be generated, but also keep the method body in compilation result.
  2. Compile the helpers to LLVM IR: clang -S helper.c -emit-llvm -o helper.ll -O2
  3. Replace define dso_local with define linkonce_odr dso_local: sed -i 's/^define dso_local/define linkonce_odr dso_local/g' helper.ll
  4. For performance, convert the processed helper.ll to bitcode: llvm-as helper.ll -o helper.bc

Now for each Rust crate, we:

  1. Compile to LLVM bc: rustc --crate-type=lib --emit=llvm-bc --emit=metadata b.rs -C opt-level=2 -L .
  2. Link with the helper with llvm-link: llvm-link b.bc helper.bc -o b.m.bc (for some reason helper.bc needs to go after b.bc, but not before)
  3. Perform the optimisation and generate an object file: clang -c b.m.bc -o b.o -O2

This way, if LLVM decided to inline the function, it will disappear from the object file entirely. If LLVM decided to not inline the function, it will generate a symbol with weak linkage so it's fine to have multiple copies.
What I am doing now is a mix of 1 an 3 I think. I compile the helpers to LLVM Bitcode but I link the module with linker plugin LTO.

I do want to point out here that you will likely want to be a little careful here - clang and rustc can only LTO x-lang safely if they share an LLVM. This may be a bit awkward to autodetect in the Linux kernel environment, as:

  1. rustc-as-distributed-by-a-distro will frequently link against a different LLVM than upstream (whatever the distro is currently shipping)
  2. People using clang from host and rustc from rustup is probably a common configuration. This will frequently be a mismatch.
  3. Some major kernel build environments, (Android at a minimum) use LLVMs at head. This means you cannot even depend on the version number in the bitcode being a sufficient check.

I would suggest that while this is a good feature, you should make this a default off config that people can switch on if they know they have paired compilers.
So I lower the Rust crate to Bitcode as well and then do LTO with the linker plugin across rust and helper inputs.
Well, any of the 3 options would require a matching LLVM.
Here's a very small example if you want to play around: https://gist.github.com/nbdd0121/d4bf7dd7f9b6d6b50fa18b1092f45a3c
This does overall seem like a good scheme though. I wonder if in the larger ecosystem, build.rs could figure out how to use this to jam bindgen-generated-code into .rlibs. I suspect it would work.
Andreas, for your solution do you not require the linkonce_odr modification?

Matthew Maurer said:

I do want to point out here that you will likely want to be a little careful here - clang and rustc can only LTO x-lang safely if they share an LLVM. This may be a bit awkward to autodetect in the Linux kernel environment, as:

  1. rustc-as-distributed-by-a-distro will frequently link against a different LLVM than upstream (whatever the distro is currently shipping)
  2. People using clang from host and rustc from rustup is probably a common configuration. This will frequently be a mismatch.
  3. Some major kernel build environments, (Android at a minimum) use LLVMs at head. This means you cannot even depend on the version number in the bitcode being a sufficient check.

I would suggest that while this is a good feature, you should make this a default off config that people can switch on if they know they have paired compilers.

How much the same do they have to be ? Same major? Same major.minor.patch? Same build output binaries?
If you're using a released LLVM, then major.minor.

Gary Guo said:

Well, any of the 3 options would require a matching LLVM.

Thanks, I will check it out. What I do now might violate ODR (which I did not know about). I basically define the helpers twice. Once for inclusion in the kernel image as exported symbols, and once for the module without exporting. They are currently different functions in different C files, so technically there is no violation of ODR yet. I do not get name clashes, as the helpers linked into the modules do not exist in the final symbol table.

But eventually I would like to not have the same C code twice. I would like to figure out a way to use the same helpers.c file for both the kernel image and the module. I was thinking about conditionally exporting the symbols if the file is being built as part of the kernel and not if built as part of a module. Would that be OK?

If you're using LLVMs that are not officially released stables, then you're not safe unless you're using the same git commit, because they don't bother to version changes to the bitcode format inside a release cycle

Matthew Maurer said:

If you're using a released LLVM, then major.minor.

We can just check for this in the build system in case Rust and LTO is enabled.

Matthew Maurer said:

If you're using LLVMs that are not officially released stables, then you're not safe unless you're using the same git commit, because they don't bother to version changes to the bitcode format inside a release cycle

I am currently using disto LLVM and rustup rustic. I didn’t check, but I think they are not same minor. I will have to fix that.

Gary Guo said:

There is one way to workaround the issue, which is to use linkonce_odr. This is entirely LLVM internal and is not exposed to C code, but we can use some hacks:

  1. For helpers, define them using extern inline. This allows an inline hint to be generated, but also keep the method body in compilation result.
  2. Compile the helpers to LLVM IR: clang -S helper.c -emit-llvm -o helper.ll -O2
  3. Replace define dso_local with define linkonce_odr dso_local: sed -i 's/^define dso_local/define linkonce_odr dso_local/g' helper.ll
  4. For performance, convert the processed helper.ll to bitcode: llvm-as helper.ll -o helper.bc

Now for each Rust crate, we:

  1. Compile to LLVM bc: rustc --crate-type=lib --emit=llvm-bc --emit=metadata b.rs -C opt-level=2 -L .
  2. Link with the helper with llvm-link: llvm-link b.bc helper.bc -o b.m.bc (for some reason helper.bc needs to go after b.bc, but not before)
  3. Perform the optimisation and generate an object file: clang -c b.m.bc -o b.o -O2

This way, if LLVM decided to inline the function, it will disappear from the object file entirely. If LLVM decided to not inline the function, it will generate a symbol with weak linkage so it's fine to have multiple copies.

This is sort of what I am doing

  • I go directly from helpers.c to helpers.bc. Not fix up of dso_local attributes.
  • I change the program text of helpers.c to not export the symbols globally
  • I link helpers Bitcode and module Bitcode with llvm-link
  • After this I use the default kernel build flow to finish up and build .ko object

Yeah, the thing to be careful of here is that LLVM has something that looks like a backwards compatibility gaurantee - newer tools can always load older released bitcode outputs (so if you generate bitcode with LLVM 5.3, then a linker or optimizer running LLVM 8.2 can load it. If you generate bitcode with a git commit between 5.3 and 6.0, it may or may not load.).

However, the semantics are not gauranteed to be the same. This means that if you do any nontrivial transformations (for example, inlining then optimizing) after loading older bitcode, something may go wrong.

Matthew Maurer said:

Yeah, the thing to be careful of here is that LLVM has something that looks like a backwards compatibility gaurantee - newer tools can always load older released bitcode outputs (so if you generate bitcode with LLVM 5.3, then a linker or optimizer running LLVM 8.2 can load it. If you generate bitcode with a git commit between 5.3 and 6.0, it may or may not load.).

However, the semantics are not gauranteed to be the same. This means that if you do any nontrivial transformations (for example, inlining then optimizing), something may go wrong.

Yea, someone was theorizing that is why my builds could not boot on real HW. I plan to redo the experiment with aligned tools next week.

If you're willing to build your own rustc, if you provide it a path to llvm-configsetting in config.toml you can use to point it to the LLVM that was used for your clang, which is the easiest way to be sure. Debian-likes usually provide llvm-config for the LLVM the llvm-dev package.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Gary Guo said:

There is one way to workaround the issue, which is to use linkonce_odr. This is entirely LLVM internal and is not exposed to C code, but we can use some hacks:

  1. For helpers, define them using extern inline. This allows an inline hint to be generated, but also keep the method body in compilation result.
  2. Compile the helpers to LLVM IR: clang -S helper.c -emit-llvm -o helper.ll -O2
  3. Replace define dso_local with define linkonce_odr dso_local: sed -i 's/^define dso_local/define linkonce_odr dso_local/g' helper.ll
  4. For performance, convert the processed helper.ll to bitcode: llvm-as helper.ll -o helper.bc

Now for each Rust crate, we:

  1. Compile to LLVM bc: rustc --crate-type=lib --emit=llvm-bc --emit=metadata b.rs -C opt-level=2 -L .
  2. Link with the helper with llvm-link: llvm-link b.bc helper.bc -o b.m.bc (for some reason helper.bc needs to go after b.bc, but not before)
  3. Perform the optimisation and generate an object file: clang -c b.m.bc -o b.o -O2

This way, if LLVM decided to inline the function, it will disappear from the object file entirely. If LLVM decided to not inline the function, it will generate a symbol with weak linkage so it's fine to have multiple copies.

This is sort of what I am doing

  • I go directly from helpers.c to helpers.bc. Not fix up of dso_local attributes.
  • I change the program text of helpers.c to not export the symbols globally
  • I link helpers Bitcode and module Bitcode with llvm-link
  • After this I use the default kernel build flow to finish up and build .ko object

@Gary Guo I thought I would be able to pass helpers.bc and module.bc to the last steps that link up the module with global LTO and benefit from inlining with that. But for some reason, linking together the helper and module Bitcode with llvm-link first is required. I do not understand why this is. Do you have any idea?

Matthew Maurer said:

If you're willing to build your own rustc, if you provide it a path to llvm-configsetting in config.toml you can use to point it to the LLVM that was used for your clang, which is the easiest way to be sure. Debian-likes usually provide llvm-config for the LLVM the llvm-dev package.

I am building on Debian now, with llvm-16 form llvm project PPA I think (on an ad-how dev system). But I just set up a CI job that builds a NixOS expression with a Rust enabled kernel, for running bare metal performance tests. I might as well leverage that tooling to control the compiler.
Upside of that is it would be easy for people to replicate the environment if they want to try it out.

Note that the version number given here is only an approximation as Rust sometimes uses unstable revisions of LLVM.. So you basically _have_ to build rustic yourself with a known LLVM to be absolutely sure that it works?

Andreas Hindborg said:

  • I go directly from helpers.c to helpers.bc. Not fix up of dso_local attributes.
  • I change the program text of helpers.c to not export the symbols globally
  • I link helpers Bitcode and module Bitcode with llvm-link
  • After this I use the default kernel build flow to finish up and build .ko object

Yeah, this works for loadable modules, but not if you link it directly into vmlinux.
Ah, I see. And the steps you suggest allow us to inline C functions into Rust code without enabling global LTO on the kernel image. Which is faster, and does not mess with boot code.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Gary Guo I thought I would be able to pass helpers.bc and module.bc to the last steps that link up the module with global LTO and benefit from inlining with that. But for some reason, linking together the helper and module Bitcode with llvm-link first is required. I do not understand why this is. Do you have any idea?

If you try to link them together using clang then then semantics would be each bc having their own translation unit, and linkonce_odr would then be discarded because it's not used inside the translation unit.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Note that the version number given here is only an approximation as Rust sometimes uses unstable revisions of LLVM.. So you basically _have_ to build rustic yourself with a known LLVM to be absolutely sure that it works?

To be absolutely sure, yes. However, I think we've been at stable LLVM + a few minor bugfix patches for the last couple LLVMs.

Matthew Maurer said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Note that the version number given here is only an approximation as Rust sometimes uses unstable revisions of LLVM.. So you basically _have_ to build rustic yourself with a known LLVM to be absolutely sure that it works?

To be absolutely sure, yes. However, I think we've been at stable LLVM + a few minor bugfix patches for the last couple LLVMs.

It would be nice if the rust project just shipped all the LLVM tools with their rust compiler. Even just as an extra rustup component.
@Andreas Hindborg your approach can still be used if we postprocess the .o file to make any non-inlined helper symbols weak.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Matthew Maurer said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Note that the version number given here is only an approximation as Rust sometimes uses unstable revisions of LLVM.. So you basically _have_ to build rustic yourself with a known LLVM to be absolutely sure that it works?

To be absolutely sure, yes. However, I think we've been at stable LLVM + a few minor bugfix patches for the last couple LLVMs.

There's been discussion of that, and it's the way Zig chooses to do it, but I don't think rustup is anywhere close to shipping a clang at the moment. You can get the LLVM tools - there's a rust-lld for example, which is just lld, as built by the LLVM Rust ships with.

Matthew Maurer said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Note that the version number given here is only an approximation as Rust sometimes uses unstable revisions of LLVM.. So you basically _have_ to build rustic yourself with a known LLVM to be absolutely sure that it works?

To be absolutely sure, yes. However, I think we've been at stable LLVM + a few minor bugfix patches for the last couple LLVMs.

It would be nice if the rust project just shipped all the LLVM tools with their rust compiler. Even just as an extra rustup component.

There's been discussion of that, and it's the way Zig chooses to do it, but I don't think rustup is anywhere close to shipping a clang at the moment.

Do you know what is holding them back? Seems like it should be no big deal.
I don't think it makes much sense for rust to become a distro of clang, TBH
They do this instead: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/master/src/doc/rustc/src/linker-plugin-lto.md#toolchain-compatibility

Gary Guo said:

I don't think it makes much sense for rust to become a distro of LLVM, TBH

Well, if LTO working depends on it, I think it makes sense.
Eventually the goal should just be using distro shipped rustc
They are under the impression that those pairings of clang/rustc should work for x-lang LTO

Matthew Maurer said:

They do this instead: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/master/src/doc/rustc/src/linker-plugin-lto.md#toolchain-compatibility

This is what I was using to select an LLVM, but you just told me it is not enough?
I do not believe it to be enough. Android is going to use the exact same LLVM, as they say is needed for best results in that doc

Matthew Maurer said:

I do not believe it to be enough. Android is going to use the exact same LLVM, as they say is needed for best results in that doc

Got it. I wonder what “best results” cover. I can live with 90% of the performance gain, but I cannot live with miscompilations.
Have you tested out LTO without Rust to see if it boots?

Gary Guo said:

Have you tested out LTO without Rust to see if it boots?

I do not remember, but it is an easy test so I could do that tomorrow just to be sure.

Gary Guo said:

I don't think it makes much sense for rust to become a distro of clang, TBH

But we can add the functionality (generating helpers.ll or helpers.bc) in bindgen? Maybe that makes sense?
Well, you still need a compatible version of llvm-link to use it
In this way, it's probably OK if the llvm/clang that Rust toolchain uses is different than the llvm/clang that compiles C code (assume LTO is not enabled).

Matthew Maurer said:

If you're willing to build your own rustc, if you provide it a path to llvm-configsetting in config.toml you can use to point it to the LLVM that was used for your clang, which is the easiest way to be sure. Debian-likes usually provide llvm-config for the LLVM the llvm-dev package.

We may have something soon for kernel development -- I am talking with Nathan about providing a LLVM+Rust toolchain at kernel.org (using the exact same LLVM they provide now), i.e. a package you can download with everything you need for a particular kernel branch (i.e. so one package for 6.1 LTS, another for 6.6 LTS, etc.) and that ensures the LLVM is the same and should work for LTO etc.
So if we get that, at least LTO experiments and so on should be easy to set up on the toolchain side of things.

Have you tested out LTO without Rust to see if it boots?

I do not remember, but it is an easy test so I could do that tomorrow just to be sure.

I managed to get time to test this today, and the kernel booted just fine, without Rust enabled. I’ll try enabling rust next and see if that works as well. Last time I tried this was llvm-15 and kernel 6.4 I think. So maybe stuff was fixed :man_shrugging:

My test machine is booting fine with rust enabled, the rust null block driver built in, and LTO enabled now. Something was fixed :clap:
have you done any performance checks?

Benno Lossin said:

have you done any performance checks?

I’m waiting for a time slot on the test bed. Probably later today.

Benno Lossin said:

have you done any performance checks?

image.png

Whiskers are 95 percent confidence intervals for the sample mean of each configuration.

The mean across all configurations:

Without lto: -0.015
With lto: -0.026

So, built-in C null block is benefiting more from LTO than built-in Rust null block is. On average 1.1%.
is this the performance you expect of LTO? so is it working as expected?

Benno Lossin said:

is this the performance you expect of LTO? so is it working as expected?

Not sure what I expected. As far as I recall I did not see any big difference with I did LTO on the modules only earlier.
Looks like I was missing the patch that set -Clinker_plugin=lto for rust code. I have to rerun the experiment :face_palm:
This is my "not working in progress" https://github.com/fbq/linux/tree/dev/rust/lto ;-)
I've found that LLVM will not inline a -fno-delete-null-pointer-checks function into a -fdelete-null-pointer-checks function. I think filtering this option out for helpers should be fine?
what's the effect of filtering out?
If any of the helpers do a null pointer dereference then it becomes UB instead of trapping to exception handler.
It will trigger a page fault instead of a panic?
But the page fault handler for the page at address 0 is also a stack dump
So it should be the same?
It's not guaranteed to trigger a page fault, because UB can do anything.
How?

Currently with the option C code reliable trigger a page fault on null pointer dereference. with the option removed it becomes UB, so the compiler can assume a pointer is non-null after a dereference.
The C code shouldn't contain null pointer dereference, but the option is added to prevent any "smart" C compiler from optimising struct bar* bar = &foo->bar; if (!foo) do_sth();

Well, the classic example is if (*ptr) { ... } getting optimized out if ptr is known to be null. In that case, it skips the branch instead of doing a page fault.

With the flag, null ptr dereference is guaranteed to trigger a page fault.
Cool :+1:
Would that really skip the branch though?
There are real world examples of things along these lines that inspired the addition of the flag.
You could have a valid pointer to data at address 0
No you can't. C forbids data at 0
Oh

Gary Guo said:

I've found that LLVM will not inline a -fno-delete-null-pointer-checks function into a -fdelete-null-pointer-checks function. I think filtering this option out for helpers should be fine?

so is the issue is that rustc emits code with -fdelete-null-pointer-checks?
Like in the C standard?
s/C/ISO C/ I think ;-)
https://lwn.net/Articles/342420/

Benno Lossin said:

Gary Guo said:

I've found that LLVM will not inline a -fno-delete-null-pointer-checks function into a -fdelete-null-pointer-checks function. I think filtering this option out for helpers should be fine?

so is the issue is that rustc emits code with -fdelete-null-pointer-checks?

That's the default. f-no-delete-null-pointer-checks add a null_is_valid LLVM attribute to all functions.
would it be feasible to make rustc use -f-no-delete-null-pointer-checks?
I guess that means we lose a few optimization
It doesn't make a lot sense to add it to Rust code TBH because in Rust you can't get null reference.
Niche optimisation for references is also impossible to turn off

So we shouldn't be worried about the null-pointer-dereference caused by Rust code, because that would mean a bug in Rust unsafe code, the only thing we worry is C code has a bug, and the behavior is different from a normal C function vs a helper C function?
Basically if the C code has a bug, the severity is then raised from kernel panic to a potentially exploitable UB. Although, I don't think there's many (if any?) null checks in these inline helpers.
well, there should be zero null-pointer-dereference in these inline helpers ;-)

Gary Guo said:

The C code shouldn't contain null pointer dereference, but the option is added to prevent any "smart" C compiler from optimising struct bar* bar = &foo->bar; if (!foo) do_sth();

Would compiler optimize with "delete-null-pointer-checks" even if it's a volatile read? Like if (!READ_ONCE(foo)) do_sth();?

In rust doing the equivalent of `struct bar* bar = &foo->bar; if (!READ_ONCE(foo)) do_sth(); could spuriously read the value behind *bar even if !foo. Creating a reference is a promise that the reference is dereferenceable. In both stacked borrows and tree borrows creating a shared reference will insert a fake read of the referenced memory. A fake read acts like a real read in that it causes UB when a real read would cause UB, but it just so happens to be lowered to a nop by the compiler unless the compiler decides that speculatively reading it would be advantageous.
I've rebased my change on top the helper splitting and get it to a working state at https://github.com/nbdd0121/linux/tree/thin-lto. @Andreas Hindborg

Gary Guo said:

I've rebased my change on top the helper splitting and get it to a working state at https://github.com/nbdd0121/linux/tree/thin-lto. Andreas Hindborg

Awesome. Anything I need to enable in config, or is it enough to apply the patch and build?
CONFIG_RUST_LTO_HELPERS=y
There're 3 patches to apply
Ok. I will queue a run tomorrow. Runtime for the full thing is 55 hours, so I should have results maybe by Friday evening.

CONFIG_RUST_LTO_HELPERS=y

This doesn't need full LTO support, it's just linking two LLVM BC modules

So it is OK to link with linker != lld after the bc has been patched into the rust crate for instance?
Yes

I got it to build, and it seems to work. There are no rust_helper_.. in vmlinux.o, or in my rust module. Everything seems to be inlined correctly. I will send it off to test now.

@Gary Guo clang is complaining about some options that it does not use when lowering from bc to machine code. Not sure how to fix it. Perhap we should just filter out those options? Or maybe we should use link time flags instead?
Yeah, I am aware of the issue. I primarily want to get hold of the optimization flags

I got this error in CI:

make[3]: *** No rule to make target 'rust/helpers_combined.o', needed by 'rust/exports_helpers_generated.h'.  Stop.

       last 10 log lines:

Interesting, can you share your setup? llvm-link is available but not llvm-as?

It is a bespoke NixOS setup. I will share the config at some point, but it is a bit too messy right now. I am using nix-netboot-serve to boot bare metal nodes into NixOS system expressions that I generate for the CI task.

I did not test LTO since I put this system online. Otherwise I would have gotten an error for llvm-link as well.

I was able to hack up something similar (don't have it broken out into CONFIG_ statements) by turning on LTO for Rust only, turning on LTO for helpers.o, and then switching all the post-processing you'd do for a full LTO kernel build on. I don't see anything starting with rust_helpers_ in our resulting vmlinux, and BPF loads, so it avoids the BTF poisoning issue Alice and I were hitting earlier.

What you have may be cleaner since it doesn't force you to LTO the Rust stuff, just mentioning it since it avoids the need for special linking procedures.
The reason that I go with this approach is that it allows inlining helpers into loadable modules.
I would expect loadable modules to usually be accessing this stuff via subsytem bindings in the base kernel image, but that makes some sense.
IIRC Andreas compile his driver as modules

Matthew Maurer said:

I would expect loadable modules to usually be accessing this stuff via subsytem bindings in the base kernel image, but that makes some sense.

They do access C APIs through subsystem abstractions. But some times the Rust abstractions are inlined into the module. If the helpers are inlined into the abstraction code emitted in vmlinux, it does not help the module.
